title: The Skills That Compound
concept: Why context and retrieval engineering remain valuable across model upgrades, unlike prompt hacks tied to one model version.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we explored 'What to Build vs. What to Buy'. What is the primary trade-off when choosing between building a custom component or buying a managed service?

options.text: How to write complex Python loops for data processing
Balancing speed and convenience against customization and control
How to train an LLM completely from scratch in your browser

exercises.explain: Deciding whether to write custom code or adopt managed platforms is a key choice in 'Build vs. Buy' that balances development speed with long-term customization needs.

cards.title: Will Your Code Survive Next Week?
cards.body: AI models get updated constantly, rendering yesterday's magic prompt tricks useless. To build lasting software, we must focus on engineering skills that compound over time instead of chasing temporary workarounds.

cards.title: Compounding Skills vs. Prompt Hacks
cards.body: Compounding skills are engineering practices, like building clean retrieval pipelines (RAG) and evaluation systems, that remain valuable and even improve when you swap in a smarter, newer AI model. Prompt hacks are fragile shortcuts tailored to a specific model's quirks.

cards.title: The Smart Lock vs. The Squeaky Door
cards.body: Think of prompt hacks like learning the exact squeak of your old bedroom door to open it quietly. Retrieval engineering is like installing a universal smart lock that works on any door you buy in the future.

cards.title: The Upgrade Test
cards.body: When upgrading from GPT-3.5 to GPT-4, teams who spent weeks adding 'please answer step-by-step' to every prompt found their hacks redundant. Teams who built a clean vector database pipeline saw their system's accuracy jump instantly without rewriting code.

cards.title: Myth: Smarter Models Don't Need Clean Data
cards.body: Many believe that because LLMs are getting smarter, we won't need to curate search data or manage context. In reality, better models process high-quality retrieved data even more effectively, making retrieval engineering a permanent superpower.

cards.title: How Solutions Age
cards.body: See how different engineering choices age when a new model version is released.

left.text: Adding magic words like 'deep breath' to get correct math answers. Breaks on upgrade.

right.text: Structuring clean database queries. Gets instantly better with a smarter model.

exercises.prompt: Why is relying heavily on fragile prompt hacks (like specific punctuation tricks) considered a bad long-term engineering practice?

options.text: They are too expensive to write.
They are brittle and break when the underlying model is upgraded.
They only work for image generation models.

exercises.explain: Prompt hacks are highly sensitive to the specific training and weights of a single model version. When the model is upgraded, these quirks change, often making the hacks useless or counterproductive.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Because new LLMs have massive context windows, we no longer need to filter or structure the data we retrieve for them.
exercises.explain: Even with massive context windows, feeding raw, noisy, or irrelevant data reduces accuracy and increases latency. Clean retrieval and context structuring remain critical.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the sentence about building long-term AI systems.
exercises.sentence: Investing in clean data retrieval is a compounding skill because it remains valuable across model updates.
exercises.explain: Building clean retrieval pipelines (RAG) is a compounding engineering skill because it continues to deliver value and works even better when newer, more powerful LLMs are released.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following implementations relies on a temporary prompt hack rather than a compounding engineering skill?

options.text: We built a pipeline that retrieves the top 3 most relevant user manuals using vector search and formats them as clean JSON.
We solved the accuracy issue by prefixing every prompt with 'You are a genius professor who never makes mistakes, answer perfectly'.
We established an automated evaluation suite to run 100 test cases every time we pull updated context documents.

exercises.explain: Option B represents a brittle prompt hack (using magic phrases) rather than a robust, compounding architectural practice like clean data pipelines or structured schemas.

exercises.prompt: Your team is preparing to upgrade your application's core LLM to a newly released version. Which strategy represents a 'compounding skill' that ensures a smooth, high-performing transition?

options.text: Discard all RAG systems since newer models have infinite context windows and perfect memory
Focus exclusively on micro-optimizing prompt spellings for the current model version
Build model-agnostic retrieval pipelines and automated evaluation suites that scale with new model releases
Hardcode model-specific responses into the application backend to prevent upgrades

exercises.explain: Investing in robust data retrieval pipelines (RAG) and evaluation systems creates a foundation that immediately benefits from stronger models, whereas brittle prompt hacks often break and must be rewritten from scratch when a model is upgraded.
